title: UN Guiding Principles on Business and Human Rights (UNGPs)
description: The UNGPs were unanimously endorsed by the UN Human Rights Council in 2011. They rest on three pillars: (1) the State duty to protect human rights, (2) the corporate responsibility to respect human rights, and (3) access to remedy for victims. The 31 principles provide an authoritative global standard for preventing and addressing adverse human rights impacts linked to business activity.

data.author: Professor John Ruggie (Special Representative of the Secretary-General)

pillars.name: State Duty to Protect
pillars.summary: States must protect against human rights abuses within their territory and/or jurisdiction by third parties, including business enterprises, through appropriate policies, regulation, and adjudication.

pillars.name: Corporate Responsibility to Respect
pillars.summary: Business enterprises should respect human rights by avoiding infringing on the rights of others and addressing adverse impacts with which they are involved. This requires a human rights due diligence process.

pillars.name: Access to Remedy
pillars.summary: Victims of business-related human rights abuses must have access to effective remedy, both judicial and non-judicial. States must ensure appropriate and effective remedial mechanisms.

principlesSummary.1: States must protect against human rights abuse by third parties including businesses
principlesSummary.2: States should set out clearly the expectation that businesses respect human rights
principlesSummary.3: States should enforce laws requiring businesses to respect human rights and periodically assess adequacy of such laws
principlesSummary.4: States should take additional steps to protect against abuses by State-owned enterprises
principlesSummary.5: States should exercise adequate oversight when contracting with or legislating for business services
principlesSummary.6: States should promote respect for human rights by businesses with which they conduct commercial transactions
principlesSummary.7: States should ensure policy coherence in conflict-affected areas
principlesSummary.8: States should ensure that governmental departments and agencies that shape business practices are aware of and observe the State's human rights obligations
principlesSummary.9: States should maintain adequate domestic policy space to meet their human rights obligations when entering into trade agreements
principlesSummary.10: States acting as members of multilateral institutions should promote business respect for human rights
principlesSummary.11: Business enterprises should respect human rights — avoid causing or contributing to adverse impacts and address impacts when they occur
principlesSummary.12: The responsibility to respect human rights refers to internationally recognized human rights, at a minimum the International Bill of Human Rights and the ILO Declaration
principlesSummary.13: The responsibility to respect requires businesses to avoid causing/contributing to adverse impacts and seek to prevent/mitigate impacts linked to their operations, products or services
principlesSummary.14: The responsibility applies to all enterprises regardless of size, sector, operational context, ownership, and structure
principlesSummary.15: Businesses need appropriate policies and processes including: a policy commitment, a human rights due diligence process, and processes to enable remediation
principlesSummary.16: Businesses should express their commitment through a statement of policy approved at the most senior level
principlesSummary.17: Businesses should carry out human rights due diligence to identify, prevent, mitigate and account for how they address adverse impacts
principlesSummary.18: Businesses should identify and assess actual and potential adverse human rights impacts involving meaningful consultation with potentially affected groups
principlesSummary.19: Businesses should integrate findings from impact assessments across relevant internal functions and take appropriate action
principlesSummary.20: Businesses should track the effectiveness of their response to verify whether adverse impacts are being addressed
principlesSummary.21: Businesses should communicate externally how they address their human rights impacts, particularly when concerns are raised by affected stakeholders
principlesSummary.22: Where businesses identify they have caused or contributed to adverse impacts, they should provide for or cooperate in remediation
principlesSummary.23: In all contexts, businesses should comply with all applicable laws and respect internationally recognized human rights
principlesSummary.24: Where it is necessary to prioritize actions, businesses should first address the most severe potential adverse impacts
principlesSummary.25: States must take appropriate steps to ensure that those affected by business-related human rights abuses have access to effective remedy
principlesSummary.26: States should take appropriate steps to ensure effectiveness of domestic judicial mechanisms, including reducing barriers
principlesSummary.27: States should provide effective and appropriate non-judicial grievance mechanisms alongside judicial mechanisms
principlesSummary.28: States should consider ways to facilitate access to effective non-State-based grievance mechanisms
principlesSummary.29: Businesses should establish or participate in effective operational-level grievance mechanisms for individuals/communities who may be adversely impacted
principlesSummary.30: Industry, multi-stakeholder and other collaborative initiatives based on respect for human rights-related standards should ensure effective grievance mechanisms
principlesSummary.31: Effectiveness criteria for non-judicial grievance mechanisms: legitimate, accessible, predictable, equitable, transparent, rights-compatible, source of continuous learning, based on engagement and dialogue

title: OECD Guidelines for Multinational Enterprises on Responsible Business Conduct
description: Updated in June 2023 (first revision since 2011), the OECD Guidelines are recommendations addressed by governments to multinational enterprises operating in or from adhering countries. They provide non-binding principles and standards for responsible business conduct in a global context consistent with applicable laws and internationally recognised standards.

chapters.title: Concepts and Principles
chapters.summary: Establishes that the Guidelines are recommendations jointly addressed by governments to MNEs and that they are voluntary and not legally enforceable.

chapters.title: General Policies
chapters.summary: Sets out overarching principles including contributing to sustainable development, respecting human rights, encouraging local capacity building, and carrying out risk-based due diligence.

chapters.title: Disclosure
chapters.summary: Requires timely, reliable and relevant disclosure on activities, structure, financial situation, performance, ownership, and governance.

chapters.title: Human Rights
chapters.summary: Enterprises should respect human rights, carry out human rights due diligence, and provide for or cooperate in remediation of adverse human rights impacts.

chapters.title: Employment and Industrial Relations
chapters.summary: Covers freedom of association, collective bargaining, elimination of forced and child labour, non-discrimination, and occupational health and safety.

chapters.title: Environment
chapters.summary: Enterprises should take due account of environmental protection, establish an environmental management system, and address climate change within a risk-based due diligence framework.

chapters.title: Combating Bribery, Bribe Solicitation and Extortion
chapters.summary: Enterprises should not offer, promise, give, or demand a bribe or other undue advantage to obtain or retain business.

chapters.title: Consumer Interests
chapters.summary: Enterprises should act in accordance with fair business, marketing and advertising practices and take all reasonable steps to ensure the quality and reliability of goods and services.

chapters.title: Science, Technology and Innovation
chapters.summary: Enterprises should endeavour to ensure that their activities are compatible with science and technology policies and adopt practices permitting transfer and diffusion of technologies.

chapters.title: Competition
chapters.summary: Enterprises should carry out activities in a manner consistent with all applicable competition laws and refrain from anti-competitive agreements.

chapters.title: Taxation
chapters.summary: Enterprises should comply with both the letter and spirit of tax laws and regulations and treat tax governance and compliance as important elements of oversight.

keyRecommendations: Carry out risk-based due diligence as described in Chapter II and sector-specific guidance
Respect internationally recognised human rights of those affected by activities
Updated climate change provisions requiring climate transition planning consistent with international goals
New technology and digital environment provisions covering surveillance, algorithmic decision-making, and data privacy
Extended supply chain responsibility encompassing business relationships beyond direct suppliers
National Contact Points (NCPs) serve as non-judicial grievance mechanisms
2023RevisionHighlights: Alignment with Paris Agreement and updated environmental expectations
New provisions on technology, including digital security and responsible AI
Enhanced due diligence guidance for the environment and climate change
Updated lobbying and political engagement expectations
Expanded scope to cover all business relationships, not just supply chain

title: OECD Due Diligence Guidance for Responsible Business Conduct
description: Published in 2018, this guidance provides practical support to enterprises on the implementation of the OECD Guidelines for Multinational Enterprises by providing plain-language explanations of the due diligence recommendations and associated provisions. It outlines a six-step due diligence process applicable across all sectors.

sixStepProcess.title: Embed Responsible Business Conduct into Policies and Management Systems
actions: Develop and adopt a policy commitment on responsible business conduct (RBC)
Embed the policy commitment throughout the enterprise
Establish or participate in a grievance mechanism or other complaint handling process
Ensure senior management oversight and accountability
Build internal capacity through training and communication

sixStepProcess.title: Identify and Assess Adverse Impacts in Operations, Supply Chains, and Business Relationships
actions: Scope the broad areas of risk based on sector, geography, product, and enterprise-specific factors
Carry out iterative and deepening assessment of prioritised operations, suppliers and business relationships
Assess the enterprise's involvement with the identified adverse impacts (cause, contribute, directly linked)
Conduct meaningful engagement with affected and potentially affected stakeholders

sixStepProcess.title: Cease, Prevent or Mitigate Adverse Impacts
actions: Cease activities causing adverse impacts; develop and implement plans to prevent or mitigate potential impacts
Design and implement appropriate measures based on whether the enterprise causes, contributes to, or is directly linked to the adverse impact
Use leverage where the enterprise cannot prevent or mitigate impacts on its own
Collaborate with business partners and sector initiatives

sixStepProcess.title: Track Implementation and Results
actions: Track the implementation and effectiveness of due diligence activities
Apply qualitative and quantitative indicators
Draw on feedback from affected stakeholders and workers
Use lessons learned to improve due diligence processes going forward

sixStepProcess.title: Communicate How Impacts are Addressed
actions: Communicate externally relevant information on due diligence policies, processes, and activities
Include information on the identified actual and potential adverse impacts and how they are being addressed
Communication should be accessible, appropriate and sufficient and not pose risks to affected stakeholders, staff, or legitimate commercial confidentiality
Reporting can draw on existing frameworks such as GRI, ISSB, or sector-specific standards

sixStepProcess.title: Provide for or Cooperate in Remediation When Appropriate
actions: Provide for or cooperate in remediation when the enterprise has caused or contributed to an adverse impact
Remediation may include apologies, restitution, rehabilitation, financial or non-financial compensation, sanctions, and prevention
When directly linked to adverse impacts through business relationships, use leverage to encourage the entity causing the harm to provide remediation
Establish operational-level grievance mechanisms that are legitimate, accessible, predictable, equitable, transparent, and rights-compatible

keyPrinciples: Due diligence should be risk-based and proportionate
Due diligence is an ongoing process, not a one-time exercise
Due diligence relates to adverse impacts that the enterprise causes, contributes to, or is directly linked to
Enterprises can prioritise the most significant risks first but should still address all identified risks over time
Due diligence should be informed by engagement with affected stakeholders

title: Corporate Sustainability Due Diligence Directive (CSDDD / CS3D)
description: Adopted on 24 May 2024 by the Council of the EU, the CSDDD establishes mandatory human rights and environmental due diligence obligations for large EU and non-EU companies. It requires companies to identify, prevent, mitigate and account for adverse impacts on human rights and the environment in their own operations, subsidiaries, and chains of activities.

phaseIn.criteria: Companies with >5,000 employees and >EUR 1,500M net worldwide turnover
Companies with >3,000 employees and >EUR 900M net worldwide turnover
Companies with >1,000 employees and >EUR 450M net worldwide turnover

scopeThresholds.nonEUCompanies: Non-EU companies generating net turnover exceeding the above thresholds in the EU

keyArticles.title: Due Diligence Policy
keyArticles.description: Companies must integrate due diligence into all corporate policies and have a due diligence policy that includes a description of approach, code of conduct, and process for verification.

keyArticles.title: Identifying and Assessing Adverse Impacts
keyArticles.description: Obligation to take appropriate measures to identify and assess actual and potential adverse impacts on human rights and the environment.

keyArticles.title: Prioritisation of Adverse Impacts
keyArticles.description: Where companies cannot address all impacts at once, they must prioritise based on severity and likelihood.

keyArticles.title: Preventing Potential Adverse Impacts
keyArticles.description: Companies must take appropriate measures to prevent or, where not possible, adequately mitigate potential adverse impacts.

keyArticles.title: Bringing Actual Adverse Impacts to an End
keyArticles.description: Companies must take appropriate measures to bring actual adverse impacts to an end and where that is not immediately possible, minimise their extent.

keyArticles.title: Remediation of Actual Adverse Impacts
keyArticles.description: Companies must provide remediation where they have caused actual adverse impacts.

keyArticles.title: Meaningful Stakeholder Engagement
keyArticles.description: Companies must carry out meaningful engagement with stakeholders including affected communities, trade unions, and civil society.

keyArticles.title: Complaints Mechanism
keyArticles.description: Companies must establish a complaints mechanism for persons affected by adverse impacts.

keyArticles.title: Monitoring
keyArticles.description: Companies must monitor the effectiveness of their due diligence policy and measures, carrying out periodic assessments.

keyArticles.title: Communication and Reporting
keyArticles.description: Companies must publish an annual statement on their website regarding due diligence; CSRD-reporting companies fulfil this via their sustainability report.

keyArticles.title: Climate Transition Plan
keyArticles.description: Companies must adopt and put into effect a transition plan for climate change mitigation aligned with the Paris Agreement 1.5C target.

keyArticles.title: Supervisory Authorities and Penalties
keyArticles.description: Member States must designate supervisory authorities. Penalties include fines of up to 5% of worldwide net turnover and naming-and-shaming.

keyArticles.title: Civil Liability
keyArticles.description: Companies shall be liable for damages if they fail to comply with due diligence obligations and the failure results in damage.

data.penalties: Fines up to 5% of worldwide net turnover, public naming of non-compliant companies, and civil liability for damages

title: German Supply Chain Due Diligence Act (Lieferkettensorgfaltspflichtengesetz - LkSG)
description: Enacted on 16 July 2021 and effective from 1 January 2023, the LkSG requires German companies to identify, prevent, and remedy human rights and environmental risks in their supply chains. It was the first national mandatory human rights due diligence law in a major European economy.

scope.from2023: Companies with registered office or principal place of business in Germany with >=3,000 employees
scope.from2024: Companies with registered office or principal place of business in Germany with >=1,000 employees

directSuppliers: Issue a policy statement on human rights strategy
Establish preventive measures (training, audits, contractual clauses)
Take remedial action when violations are identified
Establish a complaints procedure / grievance mechanism
Conduct due diligence for indirect suppliers upon substantiated knowledge of violations
Document and report annually to BAFA
indirectSuppliers: Due diligence obligations are triggered upon 'substantiated knowledge' of a potential violation
Obligation to conduct ad hoc risk analysis
Obligation to take appropriate preventive measures vis-a-vis the actual perpetrator
Obligation to develop and implement a concept for minimising and preventing violations
Must update prevention measures for direct suppliers as needed

protectedRights: Freedom of association and collective bargaining (ILO Conventions 87, 98)
Prohibition of harmful soil change, water pollution, air pollution
Prohibition of unlawful eviction or deprivation of land
Prohibition of use of security forces leading to human rights abuse
Prohibition of mercury, POPs, and hazardous waste per Minamata, Stockholm, Basel Conventions
data.enforcementAuthority: Federal Office for Economic Affairs and Export Control (BAFA)

penalties.fines: Up to EUR 8 million or up to 2% of average annual worldwide turnover for companies with >EUR 400M turnover
penalties.publicProcurement: Exclusion from public procurement for up to 3 years

data.reporting: Annual report to BAFA due within 4 months of the end of the financial year; must be published on the company website for 7 years
data.noteOnCSDDD: Once the CSDDD is transposed into German law (by July 2026), the LkSG will likely be revised or superseded by the implementing legislation

title: French Duty of Vigilance Law (Loi de vigilance / Loi n 2017-399)
description: Enacted on 27 March 2017, France's Duty of Vigilance Law was the first national mandatory human rights and environmental due diligence law globally. It requires large French companies to establish, implement, and publish a vigilance plan identifying and preventing severe human rights and environmental risks arising from their own activities and those of their subsidiaries, subcontractors, and suppliers.

scope.threshold: Companies employing at least 5,000 employees within France or 10,000 employees worldwide (including subsidiaries) for two consecutive financial years

vigilancePlanRequirements: A mapping of risks to identify, analyse and prioritise risks
Procedures for regularly assessing the situation of subsidiaries, subcontractors, and suppliers with whom the company has an established commercial relationship
Appropriate actions to mitigate risks or prevent serious harm
A whistle-blowing/alert mechanism to collect reports on actual or potential risks
A system for monitoring measures implemented and evaluating their effectiveness

enforcement.mechanism: Civil liability — any person with standing may bring an action in court requesting the company to comply
enforcement.penalties: Courts may order injunctions to comply; liability for damages caused by failure to establish or implement the plan
enforcement.jurisdiction: Initially Paris Tribunal Judiciaire; consolidated jurisdiction since 2022

landmarkCases.summary: Friends of the Earth and Survie filed suit against TotalEnergies regarding the Tilenga and EACOP oil projects in Uganda and Tanzania. In 2023 the Paris Judicial Court ruled the claim admissible but dismissed it on the merits, finding the vigilance plan was adequate. The case is under appeal.

In January 2023, a French court issued the first injunction under the law, ordering XPO Logistics Europe to improve its vigilance plan regarding health and safety conditions of temporary workers in its warehouses.
Indigenous communities and NGOs filed suit alleging Casino's supply chain was linked to illegal deforestation and land-grabbing in Brazil and Colombia. Case is still proceeding.

Friends of the Earth, Oxfam France, and Notre Affaire a Tous filed suit alleging BNP Paribas failed to include adequate climate-related measures in its vigilance plan, particularly regarding fossil fuel financing.

title: Norwegian Transparency Act (Apenhetsloven)
description: Effective from 1 July 2022, the Norwegian Transparency Act requires larger enterprises to carry out human rights due diligence in accordance with the OECD Guidelines and publish annual reports on their due diligence. It also gives members of the public the right to request information from companies about how they address human rights impacts.

criteria: Larger enterprises resident in Norway offering goods and services in or outside Norway
Larger foreign enterprises offering goods and services in Norway and are tax-liable in Norway

Larger enterprises are defined as those meeting at least two of: (1) sales revenue of NOK 70 million, (2) balance sheet total of NOK 35 million, (3) average number of 50 full-time employees during the financial year

obligations: Carry out due diligence in accordance with the OECD Guidelines for Multinational Enterprises
Publish an annual account of due diligence on the company's website by 30 June each year
Respond to information requests from any person within three weeks regarding how the company addresses actual and potential adverse impacts on fundamental human rights and decent working conditions
dueDiligenceRequirements: Anchor responsible business conduct in the enterprise's policies
Identify and assess actual and potential adverse impacts on fundamental human rights and decent working conditions
Implement suitable measures to cease, prevent, or mitigate adverse impacts
Track implementation and results of measures
Communicate with affected stakeholders and rights-holders
Provide for or cooperate to ensure remediation where appropriate
data.rightToInformation: Any person may submit a written information request to a company. The company must respond within three weeks. The right covers general due diligence information and specific information about how the company addresses specific adverse impacts.
data.penalties: Coercive fines and infringement fines; no upper limit specified in the act

title: OECD Sector-Specific Due Diligence Guidance
description: The OECD has published sector-specific due diligence guidance documents covering minerals, extractives, garment and footwear, agriculture, and financial sector. These provide tailored recommendations for implementing the OECD Guidelines for Multinational Enterprises in high-risk sectors.

sectorGuidance.title: OECD Due Diligence Guidance for Responsible Supply Chains of Minerals from Conflict-Affected and High-Risk Areas
sectorGuidance.scope: Tin, tantalum, tungsten, gold (3TG) and all minerals
sectorGuidance.keyFeatures: Five-step framework for supply chain due diligence, supplements for tin/tantalum/tungsten and gold

sectorGuidance.title: OECD Due Diligence Guidance for Meaningful Stakeholder Engagement in the Extractive Sector
sectorGuidance.keyFeatures: Guidance on engaging affected communities and indigenous peoples

sectorGuidance.title: OECD Due Diligence Guidance for Responsible Supply Chains in the Garment and Footwear Sector
sectorGuidance.keyFeatures: Covers child labour, forced labour, living wages, freedom of association, hazardous chemicals, water use, and GHG emissions

sectorGuidance.title: OECD-FAO Guidance for Responsible Agricultural Supply Chains
sectorGuidance.keyFeatures: Covers land rights, labour rights, health and safety, food security, natural resources

sectorGuidance.title: OECD Due Diligence for Responsible Corporate Lending and Securities Underwriting
sectorGuidance.keyFeatures: Guidance on how financial institutions can implement due diligence regarding their lending and underwriting activities

title: ILO 11 Indicators of Forced Labour
description: The ILO developed 11 indicators of forced labour to help frontline criminal law enforcement officials, labour inspectors, trade union officers, NGO workers and others to identify persons who may be trapped in forced labour. The presence of a single indicator may in some cases imply forced labour; more often several indicators together point to a forced labour situation.

indicators.description: An employer takes advantage of a worker's vulnerable position, such as irregular migration status, lack of language skills, disability, or minority status.
examples: Employing irregular migrants who fear deportation
Targeting persons with mental disabilities
Exploiting persons unfamiliar with local language or customs

indicators.description: Workers are lured into a situation through false promises about working conditions, wages, housing, immigration status, or the nature/location of work.
examples: Promising good wages then paying little or nothing
Lying about living or working conditions
Misrepresenting the nature of work

indicators.description: Workers' freedom of movement is restricted — they cannot freely enter and leave their workplace or living quarters.
examples: Locked workplaces or dormitories
Workers accompanied by guards
Workers confined to a limited area

indicators.description: Workers are kept in isolation — geographically remote locations, kept away from public, or denied contact with family.
examples: Remote farms or factories with no nearby communities
Confiscation of mobile phones
Prevention of contact with friends, family, or the outside world

indicators.description: Workers are subjected to physical violence, sexual violence, or threats thereof as a means of control.
examples: Beating workers who refuse to work
Sexual assault or harassment
Violence against workers' family members

indicators.description: Workers face threats including denunciation to authorities, violence, deprivation of food/shelter, or supernatural retaliation.
examples: Threats to report undocumented workers to immigration authorities
Threats of violence against family members
Threats of further deterioration of working conditions

indicators.description: Workers' identity documents (passport, ID, travel documents) are confiscated or withheld by the employer.
examples: Confiscation of passports upon arrival at workplace
Holding work permits or visa documents
Refusing to return documents upon request

indicators.description: Workers are paid less than agreed, paid irregularly, paid in kind rather than cash, or not paid at all.
examples: Systematic non-payment or underpayment
Excessive deductions for food, housing, or tools
Payment delayed for months or years

indicators.description: Workers are compelled to work to pay off a debt or loan, often manipulated so the debt can never be repaid.
examples: Charging excessive recruitment fees that create debt
Inflating costs of transport, housing, or food
Debt inherited from family members

indicators.description: Working or living conditions are degrading, hazardous, or in violation of labour standards, and workers have no real choice but to accept.
examples: Dangerous working conditions without safety equipment
Overcrowded and unsanitary living quarters
Excessively long working hours with no rest

indicators.description: Workers are forced to work beyond legal or agreed working time limits under threat of penalty.
examples: Compulsory overtime under threat of dismissal or penalty
Working hours exceeding national legal limits
No rest days or annual leave

data.legalDefinition: ILO Forced Labour Convention, 1930 (No. 29): 'All work or service which is exacted from any person under the menace of any penalty and for which the said person has not offered himself voluntarily.'

estimatedVictims.source: ILO, Walk Free, and IOM Global Estimates of Modern Slavery (2022)

title: Global Slavery Index 2023
description: The Global Slavery Index, published by Walk Free in partnership with the ILO and IOM, measures the extent of modern slavery across 160 countries. It assesses prevalence (estimated number of people in modern slavery per 1,000 population), vulnerability, and government responses. The 2023 edition estimated 49.6 million people lived in modern slavery on any given day in 2021.

title: Uyghur Forced Labor Prevention Act (UFLPA)
description: Signed into law on 23 December 2021 and enforced from 21 June 2022, the UFLPA establishes a rebuttable presumption that goods mined, produced, or manufactured wholly or in part in the Xinjiang Uyghur Autonomous Region (XUAR) of China are made with forced labour and are prohibited from importation into the United States under Section 307 of the Tariff Act of 1930.

keyProvisions.rebuttablePresumption: All goods produced wholly or in part in XUAR are presumed to be made with forced labour unless the importer can demonstrate by clear and convincing evidence that goods were not produced using forced labour.

entityList.description: The Forced Labor Enforcement Task Force (FLETF) maintains a list of entities that mine, produce, or manufacture goods using forced labour in XUAR or that work with the government of XUAR for purposes of forced labour recruitment, transportation, or surveillance.
listTypes: Entities producing goods in XUAR using forced labour
Entities working with XUAR government on forced labour programs
Entities exporting goods from XUAR made with forced labour
Entities supplying materials from XUAR for goods made outside XUAR

clearAndConvincingEvidence: Due diligence system compliant with ILO Forced Labour Indicators
Complete supply chain mapping tracing goods to specific production facility
Credible audits or third-party verification of the supply chain
Evidence that workers provided informed consent and were not coerced
Evidence of fair compensation and working conditions

enforcementStatistics.deniedEntry: Approximately 50% of reviewed shipments denied entry or returned

highPriorityEntities: Xinjiang GCL New Energy Material Technology (polysilicon)
Lop County Hair Product Industrial Park
Hetian Haolin Hair Accessories Co. Ltd.

title: EU Regulation on Prohibiting Products Made with Forced Labour
description: Adopted in late 2024, the EU Forced Labour Regulation prohibits the placing and making available on the EU market, as well as the export from the EU, of products made with forced labour at any stage of production, manufacture, harvest, or extraction. It establishes an investigation process and enforcement mechanism across all Member States.

data.scope: All products placed on, made available on, or exported from the EU market, regardless of origin
data.forcedLabourDefinition: As defined by ILO Forced Labour Convention No. 29, including forced child labour

investigationProcess.name: Preliminary Phase
investigationProcess.description: Competent authorities assess whether there are substantiated concerns that products were made with forced labour, using a risk-based approach. They consider ILO indicators, reports from international organizations, civil society, and whistleblowers.

investigationProcess.name: Investigation Phase
investigationProcess.description: If preliminary assessment raises substantiated concerns, authorities launch a formal investigation. They may request information from economic operators, conduct inspections, and gather evidence.

investigationProcess.name: Decision Phase
investigationProcess.description: If forced labour is established, the competent authority issues a decision prohibiting the product from being placed on or exported from the EU market. Products already on the market must be withdrawn and disposed of.

keyFeatures: Risk-based approach prioritising areas of high forced labour risk
Database of high-risk sectors and geographic areas maintained by the Commission
No due diligence defence — the regulation focuses on the product, not the company's process
Commission coordination mechanism for cross-border cases
Applies to domestic EU products and imports alike
data.penalties: Products prohibited from being placed on the market, withdrawn, donated, recycled, or destroyed; Member States set specific penalties

title: UK Modern Slavery Act 2015 - Section 54 Transparency in Supply Chains
description: The UK Modern Slavery Act 2015 consolidated existing offences relating to trafficking and slavery and introduced new requirements. Section 54 requires commercial organisations with an annual turnover of GBP 36 million or more to publish an annual modern slavery statement setting out steps taken to ensure slavery and human trafficking are not taking place in their business or supply chains.

data.applicableTo: Commercial organisations that carry on a business (or part of a business) in any part of the UK

section54Requirements.mandatoryContent: A statement of the steps the organisation has taken during the financial year to ensure that slavery and human trafficking is not taking place in any of its supply chains and in any part of its own business, or a statement that no such steps have been taken.
recommendedContent: Organisation structure, business and supply chains
Policies in relation to slavery and human trafficking
Due diligence processes in relation to slavery and human trafficking in business and supply chains
Parts of the business and supply chains where there is a risk of slavery and human trafficking and steps taken to assess and manage that risk
Effectiveness in ensuring slavery and human trafficking is not taking place, measured against key performance indicators
Training available to staff about slavery and human trafficking
section54Requirements.approvalAndPublication: Must be approved by the board of directors (or equivalent) and signed by a director. Must be published on the organisation's website with a prominent link on the homepage.

data.modernSlaveryRegistry: UK Government maintains a voluntary registry at modern-slavery-statement-registry.service.gov.uk (mandatory registration announced for future legislation)
data.enforcement: Currently no financial penalties for non-compliance with Section 54 specifically, but the Secretary of State can seek an injunction through the High Court. Government has proposed reforms including financial penalties.
proposedReforms: Mandatory registration on the government registry

title: Australian Modern Slavery Act 2018 (Commonwealth)
description: The Australian Modern Slavery Act 2018 requires entities with annual consolidated revenue of AUD 100 million or more to publish annual modern slavery statements identifying modern slavery risks in their operations and supply chains and describing actions taken to address those risks. The Australian Border Force maintains a public register of statements.

data.applicableTo: Australian entities and entities carrying on business in Australia meeting the revenue threshold; Commonwealth entities must also report regardless of revenue

sevenMandatoryReportingCriteria.title: Identify the reporting entity
sevenMandatoryReportingCriteria.description: The reporting entity's name, structure, and nature of operations.

sevenMandatoryReportingCriteria.title: Describe the structure, operations and supply chains
sevenMandatoryReportingCriteria.description: Description of the entity's structure, operations, and supply chains.

sevenMandatoryReportingCriteria.title: Describe modern slavery risks
sevenMandatoryReportingCriteria.description: Description of the risks of modern slavery practices in the operations and supply chains of the reporting entity and any entities it owns or controls.

sevenMandatoryReportingCriteria.title: Describe actions taken to assess and address risks
sevenMandatoryReportingCriteria.description: Description of the actions taken by the reporting entity and any entities it owns or controls to assess and address those risks, including due diligence and remediation processes.

sevenMandatoryReportingCriteria.title: Describe how effectiveness is assessed
sevenMandatoryReportingCriteria.description: Description of how the reporting entity assesses the effectiveness of such actions.

sevenMandatoryReportingCriteria.title: Describe consultation with owned/controlled entities
sevenMandatoryReportingCriteria.description: Description of the process of consultation with any entities that the reporting entity owns or controls.

sevenMandatoryReportingCriteria.title: Any other relevant information
sevenMandatoryReportingCriteria.description: Any other information that the reporting entity considers relevant.

enforcement.original: No financial penalties; government may request entity to explain non-compliance and may publish names of non-compliant entities
enforcement.2024Amendment: The Modern Slavery Amendment (No. 2) Bill 2024 introduced civil penalties of up to AUD 140 million for failing to comply, effective from 2025

data.register: Australian Border Force Modern Slavery Register — all statements publicly available at modernslaveryregister.gov.au

title: US DOL ILAB - List of Goods Produced by Child Labor or Forced Labor
description: The Bureau of International Labor Affairs (ILAB) within the US Department of Labor maintains a list of goods and their source countries that ILAB has reason to believe are produced by child labour or forced labour in violation of international standards. The list is mandated by the Trafficking Victims Protection Reauthorization Act (TVPRA) and is updated regularly.

title: EU Conflict Minerals Regulation (Regulation 2017/821)
description: The EU Conflict Minerals Regulation entered into force on 1 January 2021 and establishes supply chain due diligence obligations for EU importers of tin, tantalum, tungsten, and gold (3TG). It requires importers above certain volume thresholds to identify and address risks of financing conflict or human rights abuses in their supply chains.

importerObligations: Establish a strong company management system for supply chain due diligence
Identify and assess risks in the supply chain
Design and implement a strategy to respond to identified risks
Carry out an independent third-party audit of supply chain due diligence
Report annually on supply chain due diligence

scope.inScope: EU importers of 3TG minerals and metals above volume thresholds importing as ores, concentrates, or metals
scope.outOfScope: Importers of finished or semi-finished products containing 3TG (downstream users encouraged to report voluntarily), recycled metals, importers below volume thresholds

data.alignedStandard: OECD Due Diligence Guidance for Responsible Supply Chains of Minerals from Conflict-Affected and High-Risk Areas
data.competentAuthorities: Each EU Member State designates one or more competent authorities to verify compliance

title: Dodd-Frank Act Section 1502 - Conflict Minerals
description: Section 1502 of the Dodd-Frank Wall Street Reform and Consumer Protection Act (2010) requires SEC-reporting companies to determine whether their products contain tin, tantalum, tungsten, or gold (3TG) that originated in the Democratic Republic of the Congo (DRC) or adjoining countries, and to disclose their findings. Companies must conduct a Reasonable Country of Origin Inquiry (RCOI) and file Form SD with the SEC.

data.applicableTo: SEC-reporting companies (issuers) that manufacture or contract to manufacture products containing 3TG that are necessary to the functionality or production of those products

complianceProcess.title: Determine if 3TG are necessary to the product
complianceProcess.description: Assess whether tin, tantalum, tungsten, or gold are contained in and necessary to the functionality or production of the company's products.

complianceProcess.title: Reasonable Country of Origin Inquiry (RCOI)
complianceProcess.description: Conduct a good-faith inquiry reasonably designed to determine whether the conflict minerals originated in the DRC or adjoining countries or came from recycled or scrap sources.

complianceProcess.title: Due Diligence (if needed)
complianceProcess.description: If RCOI indicates minerals may have originated in covered countries and are not from recycled/scrap sources, conduct due diligence on the source and chain of custody conformant with a nationally or internationally recognized framework (e.g., OECD Guidance).

complianceProcess.title: File Form SD with the SEC
complianceProcess.description: File a specialized disclosure form (Form SD) with the SEC by May 31 of each year. If due diligence was required, attach a Conflict Minerals Report (CMR) as an exhibit.

requiredContent: Description of due diligence measures taken
Description of products not found to be DRC conflict free (if applicable)
Facilities used to process the conflict minerals
Country of origin of the conflict minerals
Steps taken to mitigate the risk that conflict minerals benefit armed groups
conflictMineralsReport.auditRequirement: The CMR must include an independent private sector audit (IPSA) of the report — though this requirement was effectively suspended by a 2014 court ruling and SEC no-action relief

title: OECD 5-Step Framework for Minerals Due Diligence
description: The OECD Due Diligence Guidance for Responsible Supply Chains of Minerals from Conflict-Affected and High-Risk Areas provides a five-step framework that represents the internationally recognised standard for minerals supply chain due diligence. It is referenced by the EU Conflict Minerals Regulation, Dodd-Frank Section 1502, and the Chinese Due Diligence Guidelines for Responsible Mineral Supply Chains.

fiveSteps.title: Establish Strong Company Management Systems
actions: Adopt and commit to a supply chain policy for minerals originating from CAHRAs
Structure internal management to support supply chain due diligence
Establish a system of controls and transparency over the mineral supply chain, including a chain of custody or traceability system
Strengthen company engagement with suppliers by incorporating the due diligence policy into contracts
Establish a company-level or industry-wide grievance mechanism

fiveSteps.title: Identify and Assess Risks in the Supply Chain
actions: Identify risks of adverse impacts by mapping the factual circumstances and context of activities in the mineral supply chain
Assess risks of adverse impacts by evaluating the factual circumstances against the standards in the company's supply chain policy
Consider on-the-ground assessments in CAHRAs and use publicly available and third-party information on supply chain risks
Assess own position and capacity to influence suppliers to prevent or mitigate identified risks

fiveSteps.title: Design and Implement a Strategy to Respond to Identified Risks
actions: Report findings of the supply chain risk assessment to senior management
Devise and adopt a risk management plan based on the identified risks
Implement the risk management plan, monitor and track performance against targets, and communicate results to designated senior management
Consider options for risk mitigation, including continued engagement with measurable improvement, temporary or permanent disengagement

fiveSteps.title: Carry Out Independent Third-Party Audit of Supply Chain Due Diligence
actions: At identified points in the supply chain (e.g., smelters/refiners), carry out independent third-party audit of due diligence practices
Ensure the audit scope and criteria are consistent with this Guidance
Publish audit summary results or make them available to downstream companies

fiveSteps.title: Report on Supply Chain Due Diligence
actions: Report publicly on supply chain due diligence policies, practices, and outcomes
Include a description of the steps taken to implement the five-step framework
Provide information on identified risks and steps taken to manage those risks

supplements.name: Supplement on Tin, Tantalum and Tungsten
supplements.scope: Upstream companies from mine to smelter/refiner
supplements.keyRequirement: Smelters/refiners are the key choke point for due diligence in the 3T supply chain

supplements.name: Supplement on Gold
supplements.scope: The full gold supply chain from mine to retail
supplements.keyRequirement: Covers both large-scale and artisanal/small-scale mining; addresses money laundering risks

title: Conflict-Affected and High-Risk Areas (CAHRAs) - Definition and Examples
description: Conflict-Affected and High-Risk Areas (CAHRAs) are regions where armed conflict, widespread violence, governance collapse, or systematic human rights abuses create risks that minerals extraction and trade finance conflict or human rights violations. The EU maintains an indicative, non-exhaustive and regularly updated list of CAHRAs.

data.definition: Areas in a state of armed conflict, fragile post-conflict areas, and areas witnessing weak or non-existent governance and security, such as failed states, and widespread and systematic violations of international law, including human rights abuses (as per Annex II of OECD Due Diligence Guidance).

euIndicativeList.note: The European Commission publishes an indicative, non-exhaustive list updated periodically. The list is intended as a starting point, not a safe harbour — companies must conduct their own risk assessment.

riskFactors: Active armed conflict or post-conflict instability
Presence of armed groups (state or non-state) controlling mining areas
Weak rule of law and governance
Artisanal and small-scale mining with limited regulatory oversight

title: Responsible Minerals Initiative (RMI) - Responsible Minerals Assurance Process (RMAP)
description: The Responsible Minerals Assurance Process (RMAP) is an independent third-party assessment program managed by the Responsible Minerals Initiative (RMI) that evaluates smelters' and refiners' management systems and sourcing practices against the OECD Due Diligence Guidance. RMAP is the most widely used smelter/refiner audit program globally.

data.managedBy: Responsible Minerals Initiative (RMI), a program of the Responsible Business Alliance (RBA)

smelterStatus.conformant: Smelter/refiner has been assessed and found to be in conformance with the applicable RMAP standard
smelterStatus.active: Smelter/refiner is undergoing the assessment process and is actively engaged with RMAP
smelterStatus.nonConformant: Smelter/refiner has been assessed and found not to be in conformance

auditProcess: Smelter/refiner initiates engagement with RMI and signs a Letter of Intent
Independent third-party auditor conducts on-site assessment
Assessment evaluates management systems, sourcing policies, risk assessment processes, and chain of custody documentation
Auditor issues a finding of conformant, non-conformant, or areas requiring improvement
Conformant status is valid for 3 years; annual check-ins between full assessments
Results are published in the RMAP Public Smelter & Refiner List

tools.name: Conflict Minerals Reporting Template (CMRT)
tools.description: Standardized template used by companies to collect and share information about the smelters/refiners in their supply chain for 3TG minerals

tools.name: Extended Minerals Reporting Template (EMRT)
tools.description: Template for reporting on cobalt and mica supply chains

tools.name: Smelter & Refiner List
tools.description: Public list of smelters/refiners and their RMAP conformance status, updated quarterly

title: Cobalt Supply Chain Due Diligence - DRC Context and Risks
description: Cobalt is a critical mineral for lithium-ion batteries used in electric vehicles, consumer electronics, and energy storage. Approximately 70% of global cobalt production comes from the Democratic Republic of the Congo (DRC), where artisanal and small-scale mining (ASM) is associated with child labour, hazardous working conditions, and environmental degradation.

drcContext.totalMiners: Estimated 150,000-200,000 artisanal miners in DRC cobalt sector
drcContext.childLabour: Estimated 40,000 children working in DRC cobalt ASM (UNICEF/Amnesty, 2016 estimate)
drcContext.asmShareOfDRCProduction: 15-30% of DRC cobalt output comes from ASM

risks: Child labour in artisanal cobalt mines
Hazardous working conditions (tunnel collapses, dust exposure, toxic materials)
Environmental contamination of water sources and agricultural land
Conflict financing — some ASM areas controlled by armed groups
Radioactive materials — cobalt in DRC occurs alongside uranium deposits

dueDiligenceInitiatives.name: Cobalt Refiner Supply Chain Due Diligence Standard (RMAP for Cobalt)
dueDiligenceInitiatives.description: Third-party audit program for cobalt refiners aligned with OECD Guidance

dueDiligenceInitiatives.name: Fair Cobalt Alliance (FCA)
dueDiligenceInitiatives.description: Industry alliance working to improve conditions in ASM cobalt mining communities in DRC

dueDiligenceInitiatives.name: Cobalt for Development (C4D)
dueDiligenceInitiatives.description: Project supporting responsible ASM in DRC

dueDiligenceInitiatives.name: Re|Source
dueDiligenceInitiatives.organization: Glencore, ERG, Eurasian Resources Group, Umicore
dueDiligenceInitiatives.description: Blockchain-based traceability platform for cobalt supply chains

dueDiligenceInitiatives.name: The Cobalt Institute Responsible Sourcing Framework
dueDiligenceInitiatives.description: Industry framework for responsible cobalt sourcing aligned with OECD Guidance

title: EU Deforestation Regulation (EUDR) - Regulation 2023/1115
description: The EU Deforestation Regulation, adopted on 29 June 2023, prohibits the placing on or export from the EU market of specific commodities and derived products unless they are deforestation-free, produced in accordance with the legislation of the country of production, and covered by a due diligence statement. Originally set to apply from 30 December 2024, its application was postponed to 30 December 2025 for large operators and 30 June 2026 for micro/small enterprises.

keyRequirements: Products must be deforestation-free (no deforestation after 31 December 2020 cut-off date)
Products must be produced in accordance with the relevant legislation of the country of production
Operators must submit a due diligence statement to competent authorities before placing or exporting products
Geolocation data of all plots of land where commodities were produced must be provided
Operators must collect information, assess risk, and mitigate risk (three-step due diligence)

countryBenchmarking.description: The Commission will classify countries (or parts thereof) as low, standard, or high risk for deforestation
countryBenchmarking.lowRisk: Simplified due diligence (no risk assessment/mitigation needed)
countryBenchmarking.highRisk: Enhanced due diligence with additional scrutiny

data.penalties: Up to 4% of the operator's annual turnover in the EU; confiscation of products; temporary exclusion from public procurement and access to public financing

title: Transparency International Corruption Perceptions Index (CPI) 2024
description: The Corruption Perceptions Index (CPI) ranks 180 countries and territories by their perceived levels of public sector corruption on a scale from 0 (highly corrupt) to 100 (very clean), using data from 13 external sources. The 2024 CPI found that most countries have made little to no progress in tackling corruption, with the global average score stagnating at 43 out of 100.

title: Freedom House - Freedom in the World 2024
description: Freedom in the World is an annual global report on political rights and civil liberties published by Freedom House. It rates countries and territories on a scale of 0-100 (100 being most free) and classifies them as Free, Partly Free, or Not Free. The 2024 report covering the year 2023 found a continued global decline in freedom for the 18th consecutive year.

title: ITUC Global Rights Index 2024
description: The ITUC Global Rights Index ranks 149 countries on a scale of 1-5+ based on the degree of respect for workers' rights. It measures violations of internationally recognised collective labour rights by governments and employers. The 2024 edition found that 87% of countries violated the right to strike and 79% violated the right to collective bargaining.

scale.1: Sporadic violations of rights — collective labour rights are generally guaranteed
scale.2: Repeated violations of rights — certain rights have come under repeated attack
scale.3: Regular violations of rights — governments and/or companies regularly interfere in collective labour rights
scale.4: Systematic violations of rights — the government and/or companies are engaged in serious efforts to crush workers' voice
scale.5: No guarantee of rights — legislation does not guarantee fundamental rights; workers are exposed to autocratic regimes and unfair labour practices
scale.5+: No guarantee of rights due to the breakdown of the rule of law — workers have effectively no access to rights in law or practice

keyViolations: No right to form trade unions
Migrant workers face forced labour conditions
Trade unions are not permitted for migrant workers
Migrant workers excluded from labour law protections
Workers dismissed for trade union activity

source: Yale Center for Environmental Law & Policy / Columbia CIESIN
title: Environmental Performance Index (EPI) 2024
description: The Environmental Performance Index (EPI) is a biennial data-driven analysis produced by Yale and Columbia universities that ranks 180 countries on 58 performance indicators across 11 issue categories covering climate change, environmental health, and ecosystem vitality. The 2024 EPI evaluates how close countries are to established environmental policy targets.

publishers: Yale Center for Environmental Law & Policy

title: World Bank Worldwide Governance Indicators (WGI) 2023
description: The Worldwide Governance Indicators (WGI) project reports aggregate and individual governance indicators for over 200 countries and territories, capturing six dimensions of governance: Voice and Accountability, Political Stability, Government Effectiveness, Regulatory Quality, Rule of Law, and Control of Corruption. Indicators range from -2.5 (weakest) to +2.5 (strongest).

sixDimensions.description: Captures perceptions of the extent to which a country's citizens are able to participate in selecting their government, freedom of expression, freedom of association, and free media.

sixDimensions.indicator: Political Stability and Absence of Violence/Terrorism
sixDimensions.description: Captures perceptions of the likelihood of political instability and/or politically-motivated violence, including terrorism.

Captures perceptions of the quality of public services, the quality of the civil service, the quality of policy formulation and implementation, and the credibility of the government's commitment to such policies.
Captures perceptions of the ability of the government to formulate and implement sound policies and regulations that permit and promote private sector development.
Captures perceptions of the extent to which agents have confidence in and abide by the rules of society, including the quality of contract enforcement, property rights, the police, and the courts.

Captures perceptions of the extent to which public power is exercised for private gain, including both petty and grand forms of corruption, as well as capture of the state by elites and private interests.
